coredata.dc:title: Efficiency enhancement of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells through bi-tandem carbon quantum dots tailored transparent counter electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Jialong
Zhao, Yuanyuan
He, Benlin
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Bifacial light-harvesting technology is regarded as one effective strategy to enhance the power output of photovoltaic devices, in which the transparent electrode is crucial to determine the power conversion efficiency when illuminated from rear side. To date, the efficiency enhancement of the state-of-the-art bifacial mesoscopic solar cells is mainly focused on the rational design of high catalytic counter electrode without sacrificing the transparency. The cell performance maximum through increasing electron density at counter electrode surface to enhance its catalytic ability is still a challenging problem. In the current work, bi-tandem carbon quantum dots are made to tailor transparent CoSe counter electrode for bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells, achieving a maximized front power conversion efficiency of 8.54% and a rear efficiency of 6.55% in comparison with 7.87% and 5.03% for carbon quantum dots-free device, respectively. The mechanism behind this increment is stemmed from the photo-excited electrons of bi-tandem carbon quantum dots and therefore realizing the electron accumulation on the counter electrode surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Counter electrodes
Bifacial solar cells
Carbon quantum dots
Energy harvesting
Dye-sensitized solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of transparent CQDs1-CoSe, CQDs2-CoSe or CQDs12-CoSe CEs
Solar cells assembly and photovoltaic measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61774139 , 21503202 and 61604143 ) and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology ( QNLM201702 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The images of CQDs1 and CQDs2 aqueous solutions taken under (a) full-spectral sunlight and (b) 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (c) TEM images of CQDs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra and (e) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the CQDs1 and CQDs2.

(a) CV curves of CQDs on a platinum electrode in 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (b) Schematic diagram of the charge-transfer processes under illumination from front and rear sides. (c) Optical transmission curves of various CEs. (d) The cross-sectional SEM images of Pt, CoSe and CQDs12-CoSe CEs. (e) Characteristic J-V curves of bifacial DSSCs. (f) PCE distribution histograms of 15 devices.

CV curves of (a1) CoSe, (a2) CQDs1-CoSe and (a3) CQDs2-CoSe in I−/I3 − redox electrolyte recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 with or without irradiation. (a4) CV curves of CoSe, CQDs1-CoSe, CQDs2-CoSe and CQDs12-CoSe CEs recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 with irradiation. Nyquist EIS plots of (b1) CoSe, (b2) CQDs1-CoSe, (b3) CQDs2-CoSe and (b4) CQDs12-CoSe CEs under light or in the dark through the symmetric dummy cells from two identical CEs. Tafel polarization curves of (c1) CoSe, (c2) CQDs1-CoSe and (c3) CQDs2-CoSe under light or in the dark. The Tafel characterization is also performed on the same symmetric dummy cells. (c4) Tafel polarization curves of CoSe, CQDs1-CoSe, CQDs2-CoSe and CQDs12-CoSe CEs with irradiation.

The photovoltaic parameters of bifacial DSSCs with various CEs under front or rear irradiation. J sc: short-circuit current density, V oc: open-voltage, FF: fill factor, PCE: power conversion efficiency.
The electrochemical parameters extracted from EIS and Tafel curves.

head.ce:title: Efficiency enhancement of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells through bi-tandem carbon quantum dots tailored transparent counter electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory for Deep Blue Fishery Engineering, Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology, Qingdao 266237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory for Deep Blue Fishery Engineering
Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bifacial light-harvesting technology is regarded as one effective strategy to enhance the power output of photovoltaic devices, in which the transparent electrode is crucial to determine the power conversion efficiency when illuminated from rear side. To date, the efficiency enhancement of the state-of-the-art bifacial mesoscopic solar cells is mainly focused on the rational design of high catalytic counter electrode without sacrificing the transparency. The cell performance maximum through increasing electron density at counter electrode surface to enhance its catalytic ability is still a challenging problem. In the current work, bi-tandem carbon quantum dots are made to tailor transparent CoSe counter electrode for bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells, achieving a maximized front power conversion efficiency of 8.54% and a rear efficiency of 6.55% in comparison with 7.87% and 5.03% for carbon quantum dots-free device, respectively. The mechanism behind this increment is stemmed from the photo-excited electrons of bi-tandem carbon quantum dots and therefore realizing the electron accumulation on the counter electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:para: Creation of high-efficiency solar cells is a persistent objective to solve energy crisis and environmental pollution problems [1,2]. Among several potential solar cells, bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) that can harvest solar energy from both sides have attracted tremendous scientific interests owing to the increased light-harvesting ability and power output [3,4]. When applied in photovoltaic station, the front sides of back-row solar panels reflect partial sunlight to back sides of front-row panels. The utilization of reflected light can significantly increase the overall performances of solar cells. However, the traditional DSSCs with precious platinum counter electrode (Pt CE) can only convert solar energy into electricity from front side because of the low optical transparency of precious Pt electrode. Although semi-transparent or transparent CEs have been used for bifacial DSSCs, the low electron concentration at electrode surface limits further enhancement of rear power conversion efficiency. The function of a CE is to collect electrons from external circuit and to catalyze the reduction reaction of I3 − + 2e− → 3I−, therefore the electron density has significant impact to this charge conversion process. Till now, most of the bifacial DSSCs yield relatively lower rear irradiation efficiencies by only improving optical transmittance of CEs with sacrificing the catalytic activity [5,6]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new graphene materials with lateral size lower than 20 nm, broad absorption ranges [7], tunable bandgaps and high molar extinction coefficient [8]. These excellent performances make CQDs ideal light harvesters or interfacial modification materials for photoanodes of photovoltaics [9,10]. Previous work has demonstrated that the tandem structure from inorganic quantum dots is promising in extracting charges and increasing power conversion efficiency by building band alignment from multilayered quantum dots [11–15]. Inspired by this concept, here we make bi-tandem CQDs tailored transparent CoSe CEs (noted as CQDs12-CoSe) for bifacial DSSC applications. Upon illumination from rear side, the tandem CQDs absorb photons and excite electrons, enriching electron density at CE surface and therefore enhancing catalytic performances of a CE. The microstructures, photoelectric behaviors of CQDs and electrochemical performances of CQDs12-CoSe CEs are carefully characterized to understand the promotion to photovoltaic properties.

TiO2 colloid was synthesized according to the detailed procedures reported previously [16]. Colloidal TiO2 films were fabricated by coating the TiO2 colloid onto freshly cleaned FTO glass substrate (12 Ω square−1) with a size of 2.5 × 2.5 cm2 by a doctor-blade method. Subsequently, the FTO glass supported colloidal TiO2 films were calcined in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 30 min in air, obtaining mesoporous TiO2 film (m-TiO2). The resultant m-TiO2 electrodes with film thickness of around 10 μm and active area of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 were immersed in 0.5 mM N719 dye ethyl alcohol solutions for 48 h to obtain N719 dye sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes.

CQDs1 were prepared according to the following procedures using glucose as a raw material. Under vigorous agitation, 4 g of glucose was heated at 195 ± 5 °C for 6 min and subsequently dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water for CQDs1 aqueous solution. Subsequently, the CQDs1 were repeated dialyzed by a 3500D membrane for 24 h to obtain purified CQDs1 solution.

CQDs2 were prepared using sucrose as a raw material. Under vigorous agitation, 4 g of sucrose was dissolved in 0.5 mL of deionized water, then heated at 245 ± 5 °C for 12 min and final dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water for CQDs2 aqueous solution. Subsequently, the CQDs2 were repeated dialyzed by a 3500D membrane for 24 h to obtain purified CQDs2 solution.

A mixing aqueous solution consisting of 2 mM SeO2 ultrafine powers, 2 mM CoCl2 and 100 mM LiCl was firstly prepared under stirring. Subsequently, the CoSe alloy CE on cleaned FTO glass was fabricated on a traditional CHI660E setup comprising a CE of platinum sheet, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a working electrode of FTO substrate. A cyclic voltammetry method was employed to make CoSe on FTO substrate. The potential range, scan rate and scanning number were controlled at −1.0–0.4 V, 10 mV s−1 and 1 cycle, respectively. The electrochemical deposition of CoSe CE was performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of transparent CQDs1-CoSe, CQDs2-CoSe or CQDs12-CoSe CEs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CQD tailored transparent CE, the above-mentioned CoSe electrode was used as a working electrode and the pure CQDs1 (CQDs2 or CQDs1 and CQDs2 equal volume solution) aqueous solution was employed as electrolyte. A cyclic voltammetry method was employed to deposited various CQDs on the surface of CoSe substrate by scanning from −0.8 to 0.4 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 for 10 cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar cells assembly and photovoltaic measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Each DSSC device was fabricated by sandwiching the redox electrolyte between a dye-sensitized TiO2 photoanode and a CoSe or CQDs12-CoSe transparent alloy CE. A liquid redox electrolyte consists of 0.1 M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetramethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium iodide, 0.1 M NaI, 0.1 M KI, 0.1 M LiI, 0.05 M I2 and 0.05 M 4-tert-butyl-pyridine in acetonitrile. The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of various solar cells were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical work station under irradiation of simulated solar light from an intensity controlled to 100 mW cm−2 xenon arc lamp.

Fig. 1 a shows that both CQDs1 and CQDs2 aqueous solutions are yellow under daylight. When irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp, the reagent solutions are both brighter green luminescence (Fig. 1b), demonstrating the obvious photoluminescence (PL) effect. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQDs1 and CQDs2 in Fig. 1c reveal that the CQDs with an average diameters of 2–3 nm are homogeneously dispersed in solution. As shown in Fig. 1d, two characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks centered at ca. 227 and 280 nm are observed, corresponding to π → π∗ of CC segment and n → π∗ transition of CO bond, respectively [17–20]. Fig. 1e shows the PL spectra of CQDs1 and CQDs2 aqueous solutions under excitation wavelength from 350 nm to visible-light regions. There is a broad emission peak for either CQDs1 or CQDs2 under excitation by different wavelengths along with a red shift by changing excitation wavelength from 350 nm to visible-light region. Both CQDs have a maximal PL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. This excitation-dependent property has also been observed in other CQDs reported previously [21–24], mainly attributing to the surface states of oxygenic functional groups or different emissive sites on CQDs [24,25]. The functional groups have various energy levels, resulting in a series of emissive traps. When a certain excitation wavelength illuminates the GQDs, a surface state emissive trap will dominate the emission [20].

Before modifying transparent CoSe CE with CQDs, the energy levels including the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of CQDs1 and CQDs2 are calculated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) method [10], as shown in Fig. 2 a. CV characterization is performed using a Pt sheet coated with CQDs as working electrode, a Pt wire as counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, and 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) acetonitrile solution as supporting electrolyte. According to standard formula E LUMO = - (E red,onset+4.4) eV and E HOMO = - (E ox,onset+4.4) eV, the LUMO and HOMO levels are determined to be −3.773 and −5.141 eV for CQDs1 and -3.909 and −5.186 eV for CQDs2, respectively. Furthermore, the CQDs have an ability of photo-generating electrons upon excitation from 350 nm to visible-light regions. The high optical transparency of pristine and CQDs tailored CoSe CE is crucial to realize bifacial power generation. As shown in Fig. 2c, all the CEs have optical transparency as high as ∼70% in visible-light region, which is much higher than that of standard Pt electrode owing to the metallic luster although they have similar thickness around 10 nm from cross-sectional SEM images (see Fig. 2d). Furthermore, there is no transmission reduction upon modifying CoSe electrode with CQDs. After assembling into a bifacial DSSC, the N719 dye molecules absorb photons and release electrons to conduction band of mesoscopic TiO2 and subsequently to FTO layer under front irradiation. When illuminated from rear side, the incident light penetrates FTO glass and excite CQDs1 and CQDs2, and the electrons jump from LUMO level of CQDs1 to CQDs2 and final to CoSe, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. The I3 − ions are subsequently reduced to I− ions by electron-enriched CoSe electrode. The characteristic J-V curves under air mass 1.5 (100 mW cm−2) are shown in Fig. 2e and corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The bifacial DSSC with pristine CoSe CE yields a front efficiency of 7.87% and a rear efficiency of 5.03%, which is comparable to Pt electrode tailored device (6.96% for front efficiency and 2.91% for rear efficiency). After modifying CoSe electrode with photo-induced CQDs, the power conversion efficiencies are increased to 8.54% and 6.55% under front and rear irradiation, respectively. It should be noted that the rear efficiency is enhanced by about 30%, which is attributed to the good catalytic activity of CQDs12-CoSe CE under sunlight illumination. In order to guarantee reproducibility of the devices, fifteen nominally identical DSSCs of each type have been further fabricated, and the histograms of their power conversion efficiency distributions are shown in Fig. 2f. It appears that the power conversion efficiency distribution is narrow, showing the excellent reproducibility. Table 2

The catalytic activity towards I3 − reduction reaction is one of the most important criterions to evaluate the applicability of a CE. As shown in Fig. 3 , the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Tafel polarization curves are performed to evaluate their catalytic activity. Fig. 3a shows the CV curves of the CoSe-sole CE for I−/I3 − redox couples recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 under light irradiation or in the dark. There are two pairs of oxidation and reduction peaks from −0.6–1.2 V for CoSe electrode, demonstrating the excellent catalytic ability to I−/I3 − redox couples. The pair on the left refers to the reaction of I3 − + 2e−
⇔ 3I−, and the right pair is attributed to 3I2 + 2e−
⇔ 2I3 − [26]. The peak current density of reduction peak (left, noted J Red) and peak-to-peak separation (E pp) between reduction (left) and oxidation peaks (left) are generally used to evaluate the catalytic performance [27]. The CV curve with a larger J Red and a smaller E pp indicate a better catalytic performance for a CE [28,29]. For example, the CV curves of CoSe CEs recorded under sunlight illumination or in the dark are completely identical, as shown in Fig. 3a1, suggesting that the catalytic activity of CoSe CEs is nearly unchanged with or without illumination. Intriguingly, the situation is different for CQDs tailored CoSe electrodes, as shown in Fig. 3a2 and a3. Upon sunlight illumination, there are higher J Red and lower E pp values for CQDs1-CoSe and CQDs2-CoSe CEs in comparison with that in the dark. This demonstrates the catalytic activity of CQDs-CoSe CE is enhanced under illumination, arising from enriched electron surface from photoexcited CQDs. Additionally, the CV curve of the CQDs12-CoSe electrode in the light shows a maximal J Red and a minimal E pp (Fig. 3a4). This may be due to the fact that more electrons are generated and extracted by matching energy levels of CQDs1 and CQDs2, providing many opportunities for increasing electron density through band alignment of tandem structure (Fig. 2b).

Nyquist electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is carried out on a symmetric dummy cell fabricated with two identical CEs. The first arch represents intrinsic inter-facial charge-transfer resistance (R ct) at the electrolyte/CE interface [29]. A smaller R ct means that the CE displays an enhanced charge-transfer ability. In Fig. 3b, the Nyquist EIS curves are unchanged for CoSe-sole CE, and the values of R ct are similar with or without illumination. Upon solar irradiation, all the CQDs tailored CoSe electrodes have reduced R ct values than those in the dark, and CQDs12-CoSe electrode has the smallest R ct, which is in an agreement with CV analysis.

Fig. 3c shows the Tafel polarization curves of symmetrical dummy cells fabricated with two identical CEs that can be used to cross-check the catalytic activity and charge-transfer ability. The higher exchange current density (J 0, the intercept of the tangential lines for anodic and cathodic branches) and the higher limiting diffusion current density (J lim, the intercept of cathodic branch) indicate the better catalytic activity toward I3 − reduction. Furthermore, according to the following relationships: J 0 = RT/nFR
ct , J lim = 2nFCD
n
/l, and J Red = Kn
1.5
ACD
n
0.5
v
0.5 [30,31], where R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, n is electron number involved in reduction reaction, F is Faraday's constant, C is I3 − concentration, D
n is diffusion coefficient of I−/I3 − couples at the electrolyte/CE interface, l is the distance between CEs in a symmetric dummy cell, v is scan rate of recording CV curve, it can be clearly found that the primary results from CV curves, EIS plots and Tafel curves are in good agreement, indicating the best catalytic activity of the CQDs12-CoSe CE under illumination.

In summary, the combination of tandem CQDs structure with transparent CoSe CE is an effective strategy for markedly enhancing catalytic activity of a CE and therefore enhancing power conversion efficiency of a bifacial DSSC. Arising from the photo-excited electrons of CQD tandem, the rear efficiency of bifacial DSSC with CQDs12-CoSe CE is enhanced to 6.55% in comparison with 6.03% for CoSe based device. The concept reported here may provide new pathways for bifacial DSSC platforms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (61774139, 21503202 and 61604143) and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM201702).
